It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.

For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Clicking on the green block moved it:
- Before: [38,12]-[39,13]
- After: [40,12]-[41,13]
So clicking on the green block moves it DOWN by 2 rows. The only gameplay change was the green block position.
So the mechanic is: clicking on the green block moves it. Let me try clicking on it again to see if it keeps moving down, or if it moves in a different direction.
Also, I should try clicking on other parts of the green block or nearby to see if the movement direction depends on where I click.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking on the green block moves it DOWN by 2 rows (from [38,12]-[39,13] to [40,12]-[41,13]). The green block is movable by clicking on it. Need to understand the movement mechanics.
Plan: Click the green block again to see if it continues moving down, and try clicking on different parts of it to see if direction changes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click green block again to see if it moves further
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 40, 'col': 12}])
print(f"Click green again: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"Green: size={n['pixels']}, bbox=[{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}]")

# Try clicking on the top part of the green block
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 40, 'col': 12}])
print(f"\nClick top of green: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"Green: bbox=[{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}]")

# Try clicking on the left part
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 41, 'col': 12}])
print(f"\nClick left of green: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"Green: bbox=[{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}]")

# Try clicking on the right part
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 41, 'col': 13}])
print(f"\nClick right of green: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"Green: bbox=[{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}]")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
